verses.text: Par le cri de ralliement "À bas la dynastie de David !", les Israélites du nord se sont plus tard dissociés de Salomon ( **1R 12.16** ). L'échec de la révolte de Chéba a préfiguré l'échec ultime du royaume du nord, fondé sur une révolte similaire de Jéroboam ( **1R 12** ).

20:2 from Jordan even to Jerusalem—The quarrel had broken out shortly after the crossing of the Jordan, between Judah and the other tribes, who withdrew; so that Judah was left nearly alone to conduct the king to the metropolis.
Les dix concubines ( **15.16** ) qu'Absalom avait violées en public ( **16.21-22** ) ont partagé le sort de Mikal (voir **6.23** et note correspondante ).
Le roi avait établi Amassa à la place de Joab en tant que chef des armées ( **19.13-14** ). David attendait apparemment d'Amasa la même efficacité que celle dont Joab avait fait preuve.
Le roi avait établi Amassa à la place de Joab en tant que chef des armées ( **19.13-14** ). David attendait apparemment d'Amasa la même efficacité que celle dont Joab avait fait preuve.

20:6 And David said to Abishai,.... For it seems he would have nothing to say to Joab, being displeased with him for slaying Absalom, and having removed him from his posts; and therefore speaks to the next officer in his army, Abishai; though Josephus (p) says, he addressed himself to Joab, contrary to the express words of the text: now shall Sheba the son of Bichri do us more harm than did Absalom; gain a greater party, and give more trouble to subdue him, unless suppressed in time: take thou thy lord's servants, and pursue after him;
Voir note d'étude sur 8.18 .

20:8 Amasa went before them—Having collected some forces, he by a rapid march overtook the expedition at Gibeon, and assumed the place of commander; in which capacity, he was saluted, among others, by Joab. Joab's garment, that he had put on was girded unto him—in the fashion of travellers and soldiers. a sword . . . and as he went forth it fell out—that is, out of the scabbard. According to JOSEPHUS, he let it drop on purpose as he was accosting Amasa, that stooping, as it were accidentally, to pick it up, he might salute the new general with the naked sword in his hand, without exciting any suspicion of his design.
Cette salutation affectueuse n'était pas toujours sincère (cf. **14.33** ).

Pour la deuxième fois, Joab tue par traîtrise un commandant de l'armée adverse (le premier étant Abner, **3.26-27** ). Par deux fois, Par deux fois, David tente de fusionner des partis en conflit en absorbant le chef militaire du camp adverse (voir **3.6-13** ; **17.25** ; **19.13** ), à deux reprises, Joab fait échouer les plans de David en assassinant par traîtrise le commandant rival (voir aussi **3.22-30** ).

20:11 He that favoureth Joab, and he that is for David, let him go after Joab—It is a striking proof of Joab's unrivalled influence over the army, that with this villainous murder perpetrated before their eyes they unanimously followed him as their leader in pursuit of Sheba. A soldier conjoined his name with David's, and such a magic spell was in the word "Joab," that all the people "went on"—Amasa's men as well as the rest. The conjunction of these two names is very significant.
20:12 Amasa wallowed in blood - It is very likely that Amasa did not immediately die; I have known instances of persons living several hours after their bowels had been shed out.

20:13 When he was removed out of the highway,.... Into the field, and covered over with a cloth, that he could not be seen: all the people went on after Joab; made no stop at all, knowing nothing of the matter, or what had happened: to pursue after Sheba the son of Bichri; these were the troops that Amasa had been assembling, which followed one another after him; for Joab and Abishai, with their men, were at Gibeon first.
Abel-Beth-Maaka se situait dans la région la plus au nord du territoire israélite, à environ six kilomètres à l'ouest de la ville de Dan.
Une rampe de siège était généralement une rampe en terre construite contre le mur, une tactique bien connue pour percer les défenses d'une ville fortifiée (voir aussi **2R 25.1** ; **Jr 52.4** ).

20:17 And when he was come near unto her,.... As he did, upon the information of his men, that a woman on the wall had something to say to him: the woman said, art thou Joab? she was willing to be satisfied that he was really the general, before she would impart her mind to him: and he answered, I am he; the very person you ask after: and she said unto him, hear the words of thine handmaid; though a woman, vouchsafe to hear what I have to say: and he answered, I do hear: am ready to hear, and shall patiently and attentively hear whatever may be spoken;

La femme a raconté un proverbe pour convaincre Joab de ne pas massacrer toute une ville, surtout une ville aussi noble qu'Abel (forme abrégée d'Abel-Beth-Maaka, **20.15** ), uniquement pour capturer un criminel. • Il était courant de décrire les villages périphériques autour d'une plus grande ville comme les "filles" de la ville "mère".

La femme a raconté un proverbe pour convaincre Joab de ne pas massacrer toute une ville, surtout une ville aussi noble qu'Abel (forme abrégée d'Abel-Beth-Maaka, **20.15** ), uniquement pour capturer un criminel. • Il était courant de décrire les villages périphériques autour d'une plus grande ville comme les "filles" de la ville "mère".

20:20 And Joab answered and said, far be it from me, far be it from me,.... He repeats these words, to show how detestable it was to him to do what she suggested: that I should swallow up or destroy; any in a violent and unrighteous manner, and especially a city of which she had given such a character for its greatness and worth, and for the peaceableness and fidelity of its inhabitants.
20:21 His head shall be thrown to thee - Thus it appears she had great sway in the counsels of the city; and that the punishment of a state rebel was then, what it is now in this kingdom, beheading.

20:22 Then the woman went unto all the people in her wisdom,.... Went from the wall of the city into it, and convened the principal inhabitants together, and made so wise a speech to them, and represented things in such a light, and so prudently conducted, that they unanimously agreed to her motion: and they cut off the head of Sheba the son of Bichri, and cast it out to Joab; whose face Joab knew full well, and was satisfied it was his head that was thrown over: and he blew a trumpet;

Dans **8.16** , Joab était appelé "chef de l'armée", mais ici il est chef de l'armée d'Israël. Le contrôle plus large de David sur Israël a été établi lorsque les mouvements de sécession du nord ont été vaincus.

L'ajout de l'officier responsable du travail forcé (non mentionné dans **8.15-18** ; voir aussi **1R 4.6** ) montre que David avait désormais suffisamment de contrôle sur Israël pour exiger pour exiger le paiement des impôts et la conscription de la main-d'œuvre dans tout le royaume (cf. **1S 8.11-17** ).
Contrairement au prêtre personnel de David (voir **8.18** ; **20.26** et notes correspondantes ), Sadoc et Abiatar géraient le culte public.
Le prêtre personnel de David n'était probablement pas un lévite mais plutôt issu d'une caste de serviteurs religieux nommés à sa cour royale (voir note d'étude sur 8.18 )
